observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
a. The Municipal Accountant and the GSO Head to effect the necessary adjustments and update their respective lapsing schedule and property records;
b. The Inventory Committee to finalize the Physical Inventory Plan and the target date of the one-time cleansing; and
c. The Inventory Committee to proceed with the conduct of the one-time cleansing in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 as soon as possible. (CY 2022 AAR Observation No. 1; CY 2021 AAR Observation No. 1; CY 2020 AAR Observation No. 1; CY 2019 AAR Observation No. 1; CY 2018 AAR Observation No. 4; CY 2017 AAR Observation No. 5; CY 2016 AAR Observation No. 3; CY 2015 AAR Observation No. 1; CY 2014 AAR Observation No. 5)

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
a. Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct periodic physical inventory of supplies pursuant to Section 124 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I; and
b. Require the preparation of the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) and its submission to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively, as well as to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation with the accounting records.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MDRRMO Head, the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, and the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate the 30-percent Quick Response Fund in lump-sum and present the same in the LDRRMFIP. We likewise recommended that allocations for Disaster Response and Disaster Rehabilitation and Recovery PPAs from the 70-percent Mitigation Fund be provided.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Sangguniang Bayan, and other concerned municipal heads and personnel prioritize the finalization of the updating of the Municipality’s local revenue code, pursuant to Section 191 of R.A. No. 7160.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the BAC Chairperson and Secretariat to scrutinize the completeness and validity of the required documents submitted by the contractor not just before and during the awarding of projects but also thereafter.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to require the following in processing and accounting for liquidations and reimbursements:
(Details of required documentation and controls as per COA Circulars and internal audit suggestions.)
We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Municipal Internal Auditor to devise mechanisms and consider the preceding recommendation in doing so to regulate the use of CENRR.

We recommended and the GSO Head agreed to expedite the disposal of the Municipality’s unserviceable properties and the proper accomplishment and submission of the corresponding IIRUP. Any proceeds therefrom should be duly receipted. We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to record the dropping from the books of the said assets once they are disposed of.

We recommended and the Municipal Health Officer agreed to prepare and submit a comprehensive summary or report on all donated COVID-19 vaccines received, utilized/distributed/administered, remaining, and expired for CY 2022 and onwards, and ensure thereafter that issuances/distributions/administrations to beneficiaries, whether individuals or barangays, are properly acknowledged.
We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to disclose prospectively in-kind donations received in the Notes to the Financial Statements, and record the cost of the remaining balance of said donations in the books in the event the quarantine or pandemic ends.
We further recommended and the Municipal Internal Auditor (MIA) agreed to devise control measures to safeguard the completeness and integrity of receipt and utilization of donations, both cash and in-kind, not only for COVID-19 but also for other emergency situations.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and the Sangguniang Bayan agreed to consider the lifting of the suspension of tipping fee collection from households in view of the improved lives and economy amidst the pandemic, and of the significant benefits that the Municipality and its constituents can derive therefrom. We further recommended and the MENRO agreed to spearhead an information campaign highlighting the benefits that can be derived from the resumption of the tipping fee collection from households should the said resumption be given proper consideration.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the GSO Head to prepare the request for relief from accountability and its supporting documents and submit the same to the COA Regional Director as soon as possible to relieve him and the Municipality from the liability for the damaged property.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all the concerned officials and employees to cause the immediate liquidation of their unliquidated cash advances. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant refrain from allowing the granting of another cash advance to any official or employee with pending settlement or previous cash advance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the allocation of funds for the insurance coverage of all its insurable properties with the GSIS General Insurance Fund and compliance with the guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2018-002. If there is uncertainty as to which properties are to be insured, we suggested to have the PIF submitted to GSIS containing all the Municipality’s properties and allow GSIS to review and identify which ones are insurable.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to enhance collection efforts so that RPT and SET Receivables are realized, tax delinquencies are collected, and to avail of the remedies by conducting auction sale, administrative action to enforce payment on delinquent RPT thru levy on real property or by judicial action as provided in Section 256 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the implementing offices to: a. Implement the prompt and proper liquidation of trust funds received from Source Agencies, to the return of unreconciled excess/unutilized funds. b. Reconcile the unliquidated fund transfers with the Source Agencies and prepare the adjusting entries for the reconciling items noted.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Assessor’s Office to gather the necessary information pertaining to the three unvalued lots and to reconcile its records with the Accounting Office. We also recommended that the Assessor’s Office locate the supporting documents, if any, pertaining to the unidentified lots and maintain a proper filing thereof to prevent conflict of ownership in the future. We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned personnel to fast-track the titling of all lots owned by the Municipality in order to establish the ownership thereof.

We recommended that the Solid Waste Management Board fast-track the construction of the Eco-Park in order to utilize funds for developmental projects and to avoid further payment of tipping fee at Calajunan dumpsite.
We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer and the Rural Health Physician refund the excess amount of Hazard Allowance for CY 2015 totaling ₱67,051.80.

1.11. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to strictly
comply with the requirement of RA 7160 and the NGAS Manual for
LGUs in the setting-up of RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning
of the year.
1.12. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant, Acting
Municipal Treasurer, and Municipal Assessor agreed to perform
periodic reconciliation of their balances and records to establish a
reliable and realistic basis for the annual setting-up of tax
receivables and collections efforts.
1.13. We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to
recognize in the books any necessary adjustments upon reconciliation.

2.7. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct:

a. The GSO Head to spearhead the full implementation of annual
physical count of PPEs and submit the report thereon within the
prescribed period, which is every January 31st;

b. The Municipal Accountant and the GSO Head to include the
donated touchscreen TV in their respective books and reports;

c. The GSO Head to reclassify those PPEs that are unserviceable and
cause the proper disposal thereof, if possible;

d. The Municipal Accountant and the GSO Head to take up the
necessary adjustments and update their respective lapsing schedule
and property records;

e. The Inventory Committee to finalize the Physical Inventory Plan
and the target date of the one-time cleansing;

f. The GSO Head to facilitate the property tagging of all municipal
PPEs; and

g. The Inventory Committee to proceed with the conduct of the one-
time cleansing in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 as
soon as possible.

3.9. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the
Municipal Accountant to:

a. Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the error in the
computation of CY 2024 depreciation expense;

b. Include prospectively in the Notes to Financial Statements
disclosures on the Municipality’s Road Network System, as
provided in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-008; and

c. Coordinate with the Engineering Office and/or GSO for the
necessary data needed to be disclosed in the Notes to Financial
Statements.

4.11. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:

a. Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct periodic physical
inventory of supplies pursuant to Section 124 of the NGAS Manual
for LGUs, Volume I;

b. Require the preparation of the Report of the Physical Count of
Inventories (RPCI) and its submission to the Audit Team not later
than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second
semesters, respectively, as well as to the Municipal Accountant for
reconciliation with the accounting records; and

c. Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain the perpetual
inventory records of Supplies Ledger Cards (SLC) for each
commodity/stock, and the General Services Office (GSO) to likewise
maintain stock cards and property cards for supplies.

5.11. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to record the
monetization of leave credits of all personnel, resigned or incumbent,
under Terminal Leave Benefits account.
5.12. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to take
up the accrual of Leave Benefits Payable upon determination of the
cost or money value of the accumulated leave credits to be monetized
for the year.
5.13. We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to
disclose in the financial statements the details and financial impact of
the impending full and retroactive recognition of the cost or money
value of the accumulated leave credits of all municipal personnel whilst
awaiting clear budgeting guidelines thereon.

6.13. We recommended and the Municipal Budget Officer agreed to
coordinate with the Municipal Accountant in determining the proper
account codes to be used for both budgeting and accounting purposes
based on the nature of the expense items to facilitate consistency and to
avoid the indiscriminate use of Other Maintenance and Operating
Expense account.

6.14. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to
record accounts and transactions based on their nature and in
accordance with the RCA for LGUs, and not solely rely on the account
codes indicated in Obligation Requests.

7.7. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct:

a. The concerned Implementing Offices to expedite the
implementation of the PPAs to utilize the fund transfers within the
prescribed period, and observe strictly the parameters set in the
memorandum of agreement;

b. The Municipal Accountant to exhaust efforts in retrieving the
supporting documents and expediting the return of the unexpended
balances to the Source Agencies;

c. The Municipal Accountant to send confirmation letters to the
Source Agencies to validate the balance per books of the prior years’
unutilized fund transfers;

d. The Municipal Assessor to identify the concerned agency to which
the collected cadastral costs should be remitted/liquidated, and in
coordination with the Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal
Treasurer, facilitate the remittance/liquidation thereof; and

e. The Acting Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the concerned
implementing offices upon receipt of fund transfers from various
NGAs for the timely implementation of the corresponding PPAs and
prompt recording of their subsequent liquidation.

8.11. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to henceforth
ensure that the effects of the change in accounting policy are:

a. Properly presented in the comparative financial statements by
adjusting and indicating the adjusted opening balances of the
affected accounts for the earliest period presented therein; and

b. Adequately disclosed by providing details as to the nature, reason,
and the amount of the adjustments for each financial statement line
item in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Provide appropriately the corresponding allowance for depreciation for the depreciable components of the Road Networks in compliance with IPSAS 17 and COA Circular No. 2015-008.
We likewise recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to effect the necessary adjustments in the computation of accumulated depreciation and book values for prior-year Road Network projects.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
a. The Municipal Treasurer to:
i. Ensure accuracy and completeness in the preparation of the Report of Checks Issued following Sections 43 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I; and
ii. Prepare and provide a copy of the list of unreleased checks to the Municipal Accountant at the end of the year.
b. The Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments for unreleased checks at year-end to correct the Cash in Bank and Accounts Payable accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Set up prospectively the RPT and SET Receivables, and Deferred Income at gross amount of one percent of the assessed value of real properties at the beginning of the year; and
b. Review the accounting discrepancies noted and make the necessary adjustments.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
a. The MDRRMO Head to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to process the recording of the CDRA Information System and settle the final billing with the consultant upon final acceptance. Liquidated damages must be deducted accordingly from the final payment; and
b. The Municipal Accountant to take up in the Trust Fund and General Fund books the unrecorded transactions relative to the CDRA project.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct:
a. Review further the previous year’s charges against the Special Trust Fund and make the appropriate adjustments; and
b. Draw the necessary journal entries to recognize the transfer of assets to the General Fund.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the municipal officials and employees to observe the proper use and accomplishment of the Acceptance and Inspection Report, particularly in supporting claims for liquidation and reimbursement.

We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to compute the total liquidated damages upon completion of the project and deduct the same from the last payment. Any excess amount shall be recorded as receivable from the contractor.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Implement the prompt and proper liquidation of trust funds received from Source Agencies, to the return of unreconciled excess/unutilized funds. b. Reconcile the unliquidated fund transfers with the Source Agencies and prepare the adjusting entries for the reconciling items noted. c. Require the concerned municipal officials to coordinate immediately with the beneficiaries of the fund transfers/financial assistances for the immediate implementation of the funded projects.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to create an Inventory Committee for the sole purpose of conducting the one-time cleansing of the Municipality’s PPE and direct the said committee to cause the eventual reconciliation of the PPE balances in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 as soon as possible.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality  has created an Inventory Committee for one-time cleansing but has not yet implemented the one-time cleansing.

We also recommended that the General Services Office and the Accounting Office cause the reconciliation of the results of actual physical count with the property and accounting records pursuant to the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006. The discrepancies between the inventory reports and the accounting records should be investigated, reconciled, and adjusted.
observations.managementAction: There is a discrepancy in the amount of ₱30.025 million between the books and the RPCPPE as per the latest reconciliation report.

We likewise recommended that allocations for Disaster Response and Disaster Rehabilitation and Recovery PPAs from the 70-percent Mitigation Fund be provided.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and other concerned municipal heads and personnel agreed to prioritize the finalization of the updating of the Municipality’s local revenue code, pursuant to Section 191 of R.A. No. 7160.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the municipal officials and employees to observe the proper use and accomplishment of the Acceptance and Inspection Report, particularly in supporting claims for liquidation and reimbursement.

We recommended and the GSO Head agreed to facilitate the immediate disposal of scraps from the destroyed market and municipal hall, and support said disposal with duly accomplished NGAS-prescribed forms to free up space in the municipal premises as well as to derecognize the assets properly from the accounting records.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the proper adjustments in the books to reverse the effects of the erroneous entries made to derecognize prematurely the market and old municipal hall. Derecognize said assets only upon actual disposal.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare the necessary adjustments to reverse the effects of the erroneous entries made for the settlement from the GSIS for the fire-damaged public market and disclose the same properly in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to adjust appropriately the capitalizable repairs and maintenance costs amounting to ₱6.800 million, compute for the corresponding depreciation, and apply the same henceforth.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned Petty Cash Custodians to: (a) liquidate their cash advances in full at year end; (b) ensure that liquidations attached have complete ORs and other necessary proofs of purchases/ disbursements; and (c) return or refund the unused cash to the Municipal Treasurer at the end of the year to clear out their accountability.

We also recommended and the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure that no additional cash advance is granted to any official or employee who has not yet settled his/her previous cash advance and that all balances of the Petty Cash Fund are returned/refunded, together with the liquidation thereof, at the end of the year by issuing and requiring a certification to that effect before processing a cash advance voucher.

We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Municipal Internal Auditor to:

Devise municipal rules and regulations and apply controls that could prevent the haphazard granting of cash advances not only for petty cash but also for other purposes; and

Look into the accuracy of the account balances and corresponding journal entries made from time to time.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to provide the Municipal Accountant with the Monthly Report on Real Property Tax Collections in addition to the Monthly Report of Collections and Deposits to serve as the basis for the recording of penalties and discounts on RPT and SET.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to present prospectively in the Statement of Financial Performance the gross amounts of collections from RPT and SET and of penalties and discounts thereon.

observations.managementAction: The outstanding amount has been collected as of January 2023. The LCE required the MEEDO Head to comply with the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to notify immediately the Bureau of Treasury of the bondable job-order personnel and apply for their respective fidelity-bonding. Otherwise, reassignment of the concerned personnel to non-accountable or less accountable functions should be considered. 

We also recommended and the MEEDO Head agreed to ensure that only bonded employees are assigned to positions with cash-handling function and that all current collectors under MEEDO are bonded.

We recommended and the MEEDO and HRMO Heads agreed to conduct a thorough review of manpower complement, positioning, job orders, and contracts of service of MEEDO personnel to facilitate the “putting of the right persons in the right positions” and adopt the necessary changes or updates if warranted.

We also recommended and the MEEDO and HRMO Heads agreed to see to it that personnel hired under job orders and contracts of service perform functions that are consistent with their contracted scopes of work and, if found inconsistent, amend their respective orders and contracts. Otherwise, approving officers will be held liable for salaries or wages paid to them.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the MDRRMO Head to follow up with the contractor/consultant the completion and final delivery of the project, CDRA Information System: LCCAP; otherwise, the Municipality will have to consider taking legal action on the matter. 
We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to compute the total liquidated damages upon completion of the project and deduct the same from the last payment. Any excess amount shall be recorded as receivable from the contractor.

We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure prospectively that contracts being entered into are thoroughly reviewed so as not to put the Municipality at a disadvantage.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the MPDC, Municipal Budget Officer, and Municipal Accountant to:

Monitor closely the continuing appropriations of the 20% DF;

Ensure that the total appropriation excludes unexpended balances from completed projects from the previous year and prior; and

Correct and update the SAAOB for purposes of accurate budgeting and accounting.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MPDC Coordinator, Municipal Budget Officer, and Municipal Accountant to revert the unexpended balances of the 20% DF as of CY 2021 totaling ₱1.945 million as well as the future savings to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund for proper allocation of public funds.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require all the department heads concerned to prepare, and the Municipal Engineer to consolidate and submit, at the beginning of the year, the list of all ongoing government projects/programs/activities and those that are to be implemented during the year, together with the Consolidated Quarterly Report on Government Projects/Programs/Activities for the quarter ended December 31, 2020, and years thereafter, to ensure compliance with laws, rules, and regulations, particularly COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.
We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer and the Rural Health Physician refund the excess amount of Hazard Allowance for CY 2015 totaling ₱67,051.80.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the General
Services Office or the Inventory Committee to exhaust efforts and measures to
conduct a complete physical inventory of the Municipality’s assets and submit the
RPCPPE, among others, not later than January 31 of each year, in order for the
accuracy and existence of PPE accounts to be verified in a timely manner.

We also recommended and the General Services Office and the Accounting Office
committed to cause the reconciliation of the results of actual physical count with the
property and accounting records pursuant to the pertinent provisions of COA Circular
No. 2020-006. The discrepancies between the inventory reports and the accounting
records should be investigated, reconciled, and adjusted.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned
officials to:

a. Submit a written explanation on the procurement of COVID-19 test kits that was
not supported with the FDA Special Certification and result of the mandatory
performance validation by the RITM. Henceforth, procurement of the test kits
should be duly supported with the said documentary requirements;

b. Justify in writing the non-posting of the NOA and PO on the GPPB online portal
for procurements undertaken under the Negotiated Procurement – Emergency
Cases modality; and

c. Submit the necessary documents to validate the distribution of the COVID-19 test
